old_claim.note: Joint-Stock Companies provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Medieval sea loans and commenda contracts allocated voyage risk before standalone premium insurance separated risk transfer from investment.

source_shape.source_locator: The introduction distinguishes twelfth-century Genoese sea loans from the mid-fourteenth-century emergence of standalone insurance contracts and markets.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The economic-history study establishes sea-loan risk sharing as the earlier contractual lineage from which separate marine insurance emerged.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This replaces the anachronistic joint-stock dependency with the earlier voyage-risk contract family that the source actually places before standalone insurance.

ontology_before: Maritime Insurance depended on Joint-Stock Companies even though the graph dates those companies more than two centuries after the first known Genoese policy.
ontology_after: Maritime Insurance follows the documented transition from medieval sea-loan and commenda risk sharing to standalone premium contracts.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: maritime_insurance now depends on merchant_risk_pooling and no longer directly depends on joint_stock_companies.
would_reject_if: A stronger source dates joint-stock companies before the first standalone policies and shows that they were required to create the fourteenth-century Genoese insurance market.
The maritime_insurance node is later narrowed to corporate insurers rather than standalone marine policies.
short_reason: Sea-loan and commenda risk sharing is the documented predecessor; joint-stock companies are later institutions.
